page.title: How to Hire a Non-Executive Director (NED) in the UK
page.intro:
Appointing the right Non-Executive Director is one of the most important and most underestimated governance decisions a board makes. A strong NED brings independent perspective, constructive challenge, sector expertise, and a network that creates genuine strategic value. A weak appointment adds nothing and can create governance risk.

The NED role has become more demanding in recent years. Regulatory expectations, investor scrutiny, and the complexity of modern business models mean that NEDs are expected to contribute substantively to strategy, risk management, and board effectiveness — not simply to fulfil a compliance function.

Board composition has also become an area of increasing scrutiny. Investors, regulators, and stakeholders expect boards to reflect appropriate diversity of background, experience, and perspective. NED search must be conducted with a view to the full composition of the board, not just the vacancy being filled.
considerations: Define what specific expertise or perspective the board needs — financial, sector, digital, international, or governance — based on an honest board skills audit.
Consider diversity and board composition requirements explicitly, and brief the search firm with a clear diversity mandate.
Clarify the time commitment and fee expectations honestly — underestimating the time commitment is a common reason for NED disengagement.
Establish committee responsibilities clearly — audit, remuneration, and nomination committee membership should be defined before the search, not after appointment.
Be transparent about board dynamics and the Chair's working style — strong NEDs will ask these questions and value honesty over a sales pitch.
page.salaryRange: £25,000–£80,000 (NED fee) depending on organisation size and committee responsibilities
page.searchFee: Typically a fixed fee of £15,000–£30,000 or a percentage of the first-year NED fee
page.whySearch: The best NEDs are well-connected, highly selective about which boards they join, and rarely respond to advertisements. They join boards through personal networks and trusted introductions. A headhunter with a strong NED network and genuine governance expertise can present the opportunity credibly, manage the process professionally, and give the board access to candidates who would otherwise be unreachable.
